description: Come see a talk given by Professor Ian Jauslin from the Rutgers math department! Abstract below.--Schrödinger's cat is one of the most well known illustration of quantum mechanics, but when Erwin Schrödinger introduced the thought experiment in 1935, he did so to point out a significant flaw of quantum mechanics: the wave function alone cannot be a full representation of reality. The problem at the root of the issue is that measurement in quantum mechanics cannot be the product of the natural evolution of any system. This is called the "measurement problem". In this talk, I will explain where it comes from, what it's implications are, and describe several ideas to get around it.
name: RUMA Lecture: Schrödinger's cat and the measurement problem in quantum mechanics (Ian Jauslin)

This experience counts towards the Douglass Discovery curriculum requirement. R3: Career/Discovery/Professional Development/Research. This event is part of the Douglass' 2026 Week of the Woman Series. Week of the Woman is a dedicated series that honors women’s impact and advances women’s leadership through community-engaged programs that inspire, educate, and mobilize Douglass students. description: This event is open to all Douglass Students!   Join the Pharmacy and Nursing Living and Learning Community for a fun Sip, Snack, and Stabilize event. Discover how everyday snacks and drinks can influence your body’s balance of fluids and minerals. You’ll get to mix and match different snack/drink options to see how they help the body stay balanced and healthy. It’s a fun, hands‑on way to learn a bit of science while enjoying tasty treats and exploring what balance really means. Wellness made simple.   Stop by to refuel, recharge with a chance of winning some prizes if you can come up with a winning balancing combo! description: All BME-affiliated undergraduates, graduate students, and postdocs are invited to present their research through either posters or oral presentations. The symposium is open to the entire BME department, including non-BME students conducting research in BME labs. A panel of judges will assess both posters and presentations, and winners will receive a prize! Faculty interested in participating as judges, please reach out to us at rutgersbess@gmail.com to let us know! Please use this Google form to indicate your interest in presenting: https://forms.gle/9mdozEzRJ2NRjXgP8.   To submit an abstract, we will ask for the following: 1. Title, Authors, and Affiliations.2. An abstract (cannot exceed 250 words) that can include the following sections:   - Introduction   - Materials and Methods   - Results (If Applicable)   - Conclusions   Abstracts are due on 03/01/26 at 11:59 pm. All participants are welcome to present their posters, and those chosen for an oral presentation will be notified by the week of 03/08/26.  We hope you will join us to showcase our community's outstanding research, celebrate your achievements, and foster new collaborations and discussions! We will send out further details about the event schedule and logistics at a later date.
name: 7th Annual BESS Symposium

description: CLICK HERE TO REGISTER!   The Rutgers University Honors College is thrilled to present the 1st Annual Heart & Sole 5K at Buccleuch Park!   The race kicks off at 10:30 AM sharp, so come early to grab your bib and free event t-shirt! Pickup opens at 9:00 AM at the Large Pavilion. Every registrant will also earn a finisher medal as they cross the line!   Stick around afterward for our 11:30 AM Award Ceremony, where top finishers will be recognized with exciting awards. Extra prizes will also be raffled off!   All registration fees and donations will support the American Heart Association in honor of American Heart Month. Thank you for helping us make a meaningful impact! Registration is capped at 200 participants, so don’t wait—spots will fill fast!   Additionally, Honors College students will be awarded 2 community service hours upon request!

description: Join the Center for Women in the Arts at Douglass for a workshop led by acclaimed artist Liz Collins! This unique needlepoint workshop invites you to explore the meditative joy of stitching. Collins will guide participants through the myriad of stitches possible within needlepoint and share her distinctive methodology for creating improvisational designs, treating needlepoint as a slow drawing process in response to the grid. All skill levels and forms of stitching are welcome and supported.  Only open to Douglass and Art & Design students. Free supplies and snacks provided! Supplies/snacks are limited – First come, first served. Space is limited. Please arrive on-time. This experience counts towards the Douglass Discovery curriculum requirement. R3: Career/Discovery/Professional Development/Research. Reminder: To receive DRC curriculum credit, you must complete a reflection survey. *Liz Collins is the 2025-2026 Estelle Lebowitz & Ann Beneduce-Lebowitz Artist-in-Residence. Her solo exhibition, Shapeshifter, is on view through March 13, 2026 in the Mary H. Dana Women Artists Series Galleries at Douglass Libraries. ___ * Image and Media ReleaseBy attending this event, you acknowledge and agree that Rutgers University may photograph, record, or otherwise capture your artwork, image, and/or voice. You grant Rutgers the unrestricted right to use, reproduce, publish, and distribute these sound, still, or moving images in any medium for educational, promotional, advertising, or other purposes aligned with the University’s mission. description: Tu museo. Tu idioma. / Último Domingo es una serie que celebra la cultura latina e invita a las comunidades bilingües a unirse a través del arte. Explora nuestras deslumbrantes exposiciones, crea arte y diviértete con funciones interactivas. Gratis y abierto al público. Contamos con el generoso apoyo brindado por el programa Access for All de Art Bridges Foundation. 22 de febrero 29 de marzo Your museum. Your language. / Último Domingo is a series celebrating Latinx culture and invites bilingual communities to come together through the experience of art. Explore our stunning exhibitions, create art, and enjoy interactive performances. Free and open to the public. The Art Bridges Foundation’s Access program provided generous support for bilingual text in the All program. February 22 March 29
name: Último Domingo | Last Sunday

description: As a graduate student, teaching assistant, instructor, or friend, you may wonder about academic accommodations and accessibility. The Office of Disability Services is hosting a drop-in afternoon for graduate students interested in learning about the services that ODS provides. Come stop by the Graduate Student Lounge or join via Zoom and meet Coordinator Nychey Michel to discuss accommodations for: hearing, vision, and mobility conditions, learning disabilities, psychological diagnoses (such as anxiety or depression), chronic illnesses, traumatic brain injuries, ADHD, Autism Spectrum Disorder, and more. Bring yourself or bring a friend to ask questions and learn about outreach efforts. Coffee and donuts will be provided while supplies last!   Questions or accommodation requests specific to this event? Please email dsoffice@echo.rutgers.edu, call 848-202-3111, or visit ODS in Lucy Stone Hall, Suite A145, Livingston Campus.
name: Graduate Student Disability Services Drop-In Hours
organizationNames: Graduate Student Life (Department at Rutgers University)
Office of Disability Services (University Department)

This group encourages open dialogue, reflection, and connection while using art as a tool for self-discovery and communication.

description: This co-organized event between Rutgers and Princeton University marks the inaugural meeting of scholars from both institutions focusing on Abya Yala, the Kuna term for the indigenous territory that encompasses what is now known as Latin America.  Taking up the notion of “trans-indigenous” methodologies as proposed by Chadwick Allen – that is, thinking about the indigenous beyond the imposition of colonial borders– our gathering explores connections across indigenous communities throughout the Americas, and invites scholars from across the region to participate in this conversation.  With an interdisciplinary focus, the event brings together topics such as indigenous literature, film, ancestral medicinal practices, and oral testimony to examine how indigenous knowledge systems and traditions circulate and resonate across borders, languages, and communities. We will be joined by guest keynote speaker, poet and professor Odi González (NYU), who will address the complex process of translating oral Quechua literature into written languages, such as Spanish and English. The event will finish with a film screening of Javier Corcuera's Sigo siendo (2013), a documentary which dives into the heterogenous musical expressions of Peru, influenced by local cultures and repertoires from both indigenous and Afro-Peruvian roots. With a focus on migrant subjects in the industrial capital, the film highlights the impossibility of conceiving contemporary indigenous societies in isolation, celebrating the diverse community ties they create as they work to maintain their cultural vibrancy.
name: Trans-Indigenous Abya Yala  1st Dialogue in Indigenous Studies at Rutgers-Princeton University
organizationName: Literary Studies in Spanish Organization (Graduate Organization)

description: Drop-in for an Artful Saturday monthly event hosted by the Center for the Women in the Arts at Douglass and Scarlet Arts RX! Join us for a Spring painting picnic in Jameson courtyard (rain location: Ludwig Center). Artsy door prizes! Bring a friend and a blanket to sit on (if you have one)! Taught by a Scarlet Arts Rx student teaching artist from Rutgers Mason Gross School of the Arts. Free supplies, snacks, and boba provided! Supplies are limited – First come, first served. All skill levels and walk-ins welcome! Foundational art skills you will learn at this event: Painting and referencing nature, Color theory and color relationships. description: Come join us on Sunday, April 19, for Haru's biggest event of the semester, Spring 2026 Showcase! Our goal is to share Korean pop culture through the art of dance. This event is open to everyone: general members, families, Rutgers students, and non-Rutgers students! Our showcase is our biggest event, where our members get to showcase a multitude of K-pop performances they’ve been preparing for months for our audience. There are two acts with an intermission, which includes a raffle with a bunch of prizes our audience can partake in and possibly win big! And the best part is that admission is FREE for all!  You don't have to be performing to attend, so come out and support our performers! ♡ Let's cheer each other on and enjoy each other's company! You may also get some sweet treats, snacks, and refreshments from us to enjoy while watching performances 👀 Our showcase is also livestreamed on our YouTube page for audience members who cannot attend in person, but would love to support our performers! If you're watching from online, thank you for supporting Haru Dance ♡ More information on parking will be detailed here later in the semester. Contact us directly on IG: @harudance_official or Email: haru.kdcc@gmail.com if you have any questions! Can't wait to see y'all soon!
location: Cook/Douglass - Trayes Hall Douglass Student Center
name: HARU Spring 2026 Showcase
